topic: The Gravity Paradox — first adversarial review
our_argument_summary: Section 1.7 argues that the dome model uses gravitational physics in ~49% of its predictions but has no gravity theory. It borrows WGS-84 constants wholesale, applies them to flat-disc coordinates, and never resolves the contradiction: a flat disc with the dome's exponential ceiling profile predicts gravity dropping to ~10% at the rim, while observations show <0.6% variation. Cross-references self-contradictions 2.3 (rim gravity) and 2.8 (gradient direction), and links to Part 2b (code analysis) and Part 7 (external tests).

holes_found.description: Hardcoded WIN count '67' appears three times in the section body ('across the dome's 67 WINs', 'one-third of the 67 WINs', etc.). The current WIN count is 70 (and rising). This violates the project's own cardinal rule: never hardcode a number that can be derived from data. A dome defender can point to the stale count to imply sloppy methodology — ironic given we criticize the dome for exactly this practice.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace all instances of '67' with {{TOTAL_WINS}} template variable. The heading '49%' should also be computed dynamically or removed since the denominator is changing.

holes_found.description: Fraction mismatch in concluding paragraph. The section heading says 'Gravity Pervades 49% of the Model's Predictions' (correct: 33/67 ≈ 49%). But the closing summary says 'Roughly one-third of the 67 WINs make explicit gravity claims' — 14/67 = 20.9%, which is roughly one-fifth, not one-third. It then says 'roughly two-thirds depend on gravitational physics to function' — but 33/67 = 49%, which is roughly half, not two-thirds. The heading and the closing paragraph contradict each other. A defender will quote the inconsistency.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix fractions to match the data: 14 out of N WINs (~21%) make explicit gravity claims; 33 out of N (~49%) depend on gravitational physics directly or indirectly. Or rephrase: 'roughly one in five WINs makes explicit gravity claims; roughly half depend on gravitational physics.' Remove the 'one-third' and 'two-thirds' language entirely.

holes_found.description: Broken cross-reference tab link. The paragraph references Part 2b with onclick='showTab("pages")' but Part 2b is on the 'selftest' tab (confirmed by v6-restructure-map.json tab_ids: part2b → selftest). The 'pages' tab is Part 4 (Live Power Dashboard). Clicking this link would navigate to the wrong tab.
holes_found.recommendation: Change showTab('pages') to showTab('selftest') in the Part 2b link in the final paragraph.

holes_found.description: The ~19 indirect-gravity WINs are not enumerated. The section asserts '~19 WINs rely on gravity indirectly' but never lists them. A defender could dispute the count — arguing some of those 19 don't actually require gravity — and the reader has no way to verify the claim. This is the same vagueness we criticize in the dome's methodology.
holes_found.recommendation: Either enumerate the 19 indirect WINs (in a collapsible detail block to avoid clutter) or provide the criteria used to classify them (e.g., 'any WIN whose underlying phenomenon requires hydrostatic equilibrium, orbital mechanics, or gravitational potential'). Even a footnote listing WIN numbers would suffice.

holes_found.description: Exponential calculation imprecision. Section says exp(−20015/8619) ≈ 0.095. Actual value is 0.0981 — a 3.3% discrepancy. This makes the 'factor of 150' claim slightly understated (actual factor ≈ 170). Not a material error but imprecise for a review that emphasizes numerical rigor.
holes_found.recommendation: Use 0.098 (or 'roughly 10%') and update the factor to 'approximately 170×' or 'more than 150×'.

holes_found.description: Section says WGS84 constants 'describe the centrifugal acceleration and mass distribution of a rotating sphere, not a flat disc.' WGS84 models an oblate rotating spheroid (equatorial radius 6,378.1 km, polar radius 6,356.8 km), not a sphere. Section 1.3 correctly states 'oblate spheroid.' Using 'sphere' here is technically imprecise — a defender could argue the sloppiness undermines our credibility on this specific point.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'rotating sphere' to 'rotating oblate spheroid' for consistency with Section 1.3.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome author's own OPEN-001 ('Dome-native coordinates without WGS84 — IN PROGRESS') explicitly acknowledges the WGS84 dependency. This is a powerful self-admission: the model's creator recognizes that his predictions are built on globe-derived coordinates. Section 1.7 should quote OPEN-001 directly as evidence that even the dome author considers this an unresolved foundational problem.

The section could strengthen the 'no derivation' argument by noting that the dome's κ = 1.672 nT/μGal coupling constant requires a specific dimensional relationship between electromagnetism and gravity that does not exist in any known physical framework — not even speculative ones like Kaluza-Klein theory or string theory. The dome doesn't just lack a derivation; it proposes a dimensional coupling (nanotesla per microgal) that has no theoretical precedent.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies that geomagnetic and gravitational signals correlate during extreme events (e.g., the 2003 Halloween storm). It also correctly notes that standard physics doesn't have a single unified field theory for electromagnetism and gravity. The attempt to build a quantitative gravity framework — κ, the g(r) formula, tidal period matching — is more ambitious than typical flat-earth claims.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The correlation is explained by standard physics: both magnetic and gravity instruments respond to outer-core dynamics and storm-driven artifacts (ground currents, thermal effects). Identifying a correlation is not the same as explaining its cause. The dome names the correlation ('aetheric coupling') without deriving it from first principles, which means it can accommodate any future measurement by adjusting the aetheric density profile. An unfalsifiable explanation is not a theory.

advocate_mode.strongest_dome_defense: A dome defender would argue: (1) We DO have a gravity theory — the aetheric medium creates downward pressure, and κ = 1.672 quantifies the coupling. (2) The WGS84 constants are an interim approximation acknowledged by OPEN-001; the dome is working toward dome-native coordinates. (3) The 90% rim-gravity problem assumes g ∝ H(r), which is your approximation, not ours — our model has additional aetheric density profiles that flatten the gravity field. (4) Standard physics also can't derive g from first principles — it measures g and fits a formula (Somigliana), so demanding first-principles derivation from the dome is a double standard.
advocate_mode.our_rebuttal: (1) κ is a ratio of two measurements, not a theory — it doesn't predict what κ should be from dome geometry. (2) OPEN-001 has been 'IN PROGRESS' since at least V50.6 with no resolution — acknowledging a problem is not solving it. (3) If additional aetheric profiles flatten gravity, those profiles are additional fitted parameters, contradicting the 'zero fitted parameters' claim. (4) Standard physics derives g from Newton's law of gravitation + measured mass/radius — the Somigliana formula is the result of that derivation, not an arbitrary fit. The dome has no analogous derivation.

cross_win_consistency.issues_found: The 14 gravity-direct WINs listed in the section match the data in wins.json. All 14 are present and correctly categorized. No additional gravity-direct WINs were found that should be included but aren't. However, the WIN count (70 total, not 67) means the '49%' heading may now be 33/70 = 47%, still close but drifting.

tldr_review.notes: TLDR correctly states '14 WINs make direct gravity claims; 19 more use gravity implicitly' and 'The model depends on the very physics its geometry contradicts.' Both match the body content. The TLDR uses 'roughly half its predictions' which matches the 49% heading, not the erroneous 'two-thirds' in the concluding paragraph. TLDR is good.

deeper_analysis: Section 1.7 is structurally sound and makes a genuine, important argument — the gravity paradox IS the dome model's deepest structural weakness. The section correctly identifies the three pillars: (1) no derivation, (2) borrowed constants, (3) geometric contradiction. The cross-references to SC 2.3, SC 2.8, Part 2b, and Part 7 create a well-networked argument. However, the major issues (hardcoded counts, fraction mismatch) undermine the section's credibility on precisely the point where we claim numerical rigor. A dome defender's most effective attack would be: 'They can't even count their own WINs consistently — why should I trust their gravity calculations?' Fix the counts and fractions, add the OPEN-001 quote, and this section becomes bulletproof.

citations_verified: WGS84 Somigliana formula constants (9.7803, 0.005307, 0.0000058) — correct to standard approximation
Cross-references to SC 2.3, SC 2.8 — both exist in sections.json under part2
Dome parameters (disc_radius=20015, H0=8537, L=8619) — match raw-text/05-model.txt
citations_failed: Part 2b cross-reference uses wrong tab (showTab('pages') should be showTab('selftest'))
citations_unchecked: Van Camp et al. (2001) and Sun et al. (2010) — referenced indirectly via Section 1.6, not directly cited in 1.7
Sutton et al. (2005), Bruinsma et al. (2006) — referenced in 1.6, not 1.7
